title: Lemon curd tart
subtitle: A sharp, silky lemon custard in a crisp sweet pastry case
excerpt: A brilliant yellow lemon custard poured into a crisp, buttery pastry case and baked until just set. The filling is more curd than custard: sharp, dense, and intensely lemon, made with a high ratio of egg and butter.
sourceNotes: Lemon curd tart is a British baking tradition descended from the lemon cheesecake and lemon pie documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. The high-butter curd filling in a sweet pastry case became a British and French patisserie standard in the twentieth century, with the tarte au citron in its modern French form documented in Auguste Escoffier's successors. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records the lemon filling in pastry tradition.

content.text: A lemon curd tart works on contrast: a crisp, buttery pastry case against a filling that is dense, sharp, and barely set. The filling is closer to a lemon curd than a typical custard. It uses more butter and less cream than a crème brûlée base and sets to a silky, sliceable consistency. Getting it right depends on baking it slowly at a low temperature and taking it out while it still has a slight wobble in the centre.

and fully cooked before the filling is added. This is non-negotiable: a wet lemon filling on an under-baked case produces a soggy, pale base. The tart must cool completely before slicing; warm, the filling is too loose.

attrs.prepNote: cold, for the pastry; plus 100 g for the filling
finely grated zest and juice for the filling (about 150 ml juice)

content.text: Make the pastry and blind bake

Rub the cold pastry-quantity {{unsalted-butter}} into {{plain-flour}} and {{icing-sugar}} until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Mix in the pastry {{egg-yolks}} with 2 tablespoons of cold water to form a firm dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 60 minutes. Roll to 3 mm thick and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line the pastry with paper and fill with baking beans. Blind bake for 15 minutes, then remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 10 minutes until the base is dry and pale gold. Leave to cool.

Reduce the oven to 160°C fan (180°C conventional). Whisk {{caster-sugar}}, {{eggs}}, the zest and juice from {{lemon}}, and {{double-cream}} together. Melt the filling-quantity {{unsalted-butter}} and whisk it in. Pour the filling into the cooled pastry case, do this with the case already on the oven shelf to avoid spilling. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the filling is set at the edges with a wobble of about 5 cm in the centre. It will read 70 to 72°C on a probe thermometer at the edge. Do not overbake: a set, pale filling has been overbaked. Cool at room temperature for two hours before refrigerating.
An oven thermometer is important here. Even 10°C too high and the filling will scramble at the edges before the centre sets.
For a sharper, more classic result: use the full juice of the lemons and omit the double cream. The texture is slightly less silky but the flavour is brighter.
Serve with crème fraîche, not whipped cream; the slight acidity of crème fraîche matches the tart rather than dulling it.
